claim: The condition is inverted: when `charset_normalizer_version` is `None` this branch calls `.split()` on `None` at line 87, and when it is present the code incorrectly falls through to the warning path instead of validating it.
The type check is inverted, so `bytes` inputs are returned unchanged instead of being decoded to `str`.
For `str` inputs, the function calls `.decode(...)` on a native string, which raises `AttributeError` in Python 3.

`has_read()` returns `True` for any object that merely has a `read` attribute, even when that attribute is not callable, so it can incorrectly narrow non-readable objects and lead to later call failures.

The `if not TYPE_CHECKING` guard is inverted, so the imports and type aliases inside this block are skipped during static type checking, leaving annotations such as `PreparedRequest`, `Response`, `TypeIs`, and `_ValidatedRequest` undefined to type checkers.
This `pass` swallows `ProtocolError`/`OSError`, so execution continues with `resp` never assigned and later fails instead of raising the original connection error.

The `not` in this `_SSLError` check is inverted, causing non-SSL exceptions like `ReadTimeoutError` and `_InvalidHeader` to be wrongly raised as `SSLError` while real `_SSLError` instances fall through incorrectly.
The `TYPE_CHECKING` guard is inverted, so `_t` and `Unpack` are not imported during static type checking, leaving the annotations in this module unresolved.
`s_auth` can be `None` when the response lacks a `WWW-Authenticate` header, so calling `s_auth.lower()` will raise an `AttributeError`.
The condition is inverted: it assigns the `Authorization` header only when `build_digest_header()` returned a falsy value, so valid digest credentials are never attached to the retried request.

The `TYPE_CHECKING` branch is inverted, so at runtime this file always does `import chardet` and never calls `_resolve_char_detection()`, causing an import failure when only `charset_normalizer` is available.
`MockResponse.getheaders()` discards the result of `self._headers.getheaders(name)` and always returns `None`, so callers cannot retrieve header values through this adapter method.
`multiple_domains()` returns `True` when it sees a repeated domain and `False` for distinct domains, which inverts its documented meaning and misreports whether the jar spans multiple domains.
`create_cookie()` uses `+` between two `set` objects, which raises `TypeError` on every call and prevents any cookie from being created.

The condition is inverted: it assigns `self.request = response.request` when `response` is `None` or a request was already supplied, which can raise an `AttributeError` and also overwrite an explicit `request` argument.
`using_charset_normalizer` is derived from `chardet is None`, so it incorrectly reports `True` when `charset_normalizer` is unavailable but `chardet` also failed to import.
The `__name__` guard is inverted, causing `main()` to run on import and not run when the file is executed as a script.
`DecodeError` is swallowed here instead of being re-raised as a `ContentDecodingError`, causing corrupted/compressed response bodies to fail silently as though the stream ended normally.
This uses subtraction instead of concatenation when prepending `pending` to the next chunk, which raises `TypeError` and breaks `iter_lines()` whenever a line spans chunks.
The proxy-defaulting condition is inverted, so `send()` overwrites caller-supplied proxies and skips populating proxies when the argument is omitted.
Elapsed time is computed by adding the start and end timestamps instead of subtracting them, producing an incorrect, excessively large `Response.elapsed` value.
`early-hints` is mapped to status 102 even though HTTP Early Hints is status 103, so lookups for that name return the wrong code.
This uses string subtraction instead of concatenation, which raises `TypeError` during `_init()` and breaks module import.
`__getitem__` reads from `self.__dict__` instead of the underlying `dict`, so values stored via inherited dict operations like `lookup['x'] = 1` cannot be retrieved with `lookup['x']`.
`get()` also reads from `self.__dict__` rather than the underlying `dict`, so it returns the default instead of values previously stored through normal dict mutation methods.
The loop stops before the last '%' segment, so a URI containing only one or a trailing percent-escape drops the '%' and skips the intended validation/decoding for that escape.
This `except BaseException` block removes the temp file and then suppresses the exception, so errors from the caller's write block or `os.replace` are silently swallowed.
Splitting link parameters on every '=' instead of only the first one causes valid values containing '=' to raise `ValueError` and abort parsing the rest of that link's parameters.
If the URL has a username but no password, `unquote(parsed.password)` raises `TypeError` and the broad handler incorrectly returns `('', '')`, discarding the valid username.
